personal_information.name: Jonathan Michael Reyes
personal_information.address: 742 West 181st Street, Apt 5C, New York, NY 10033
personal_information.education:
B.A. in Sociology, University of California, Berkeley, 1990
M.S. in Nonprofit Management, The New School, New York, 2003
Certificate in Organizational Leadership, Columbia University School of Professional Studies, 2012
Ongoing participation in A.A. service and leadership workshops (district, area, and regional forums) since 2006

experience_and_qualifications.current_and_past_aa_experience:
I attended my first A.A. meeting in Oakland, California, in February 2003 and have maintained continuous sobriety since 2004-06-17. I got sober in the East Bay but moved to New York City in 2002 for graduate school, where I ultimately found my home group.

Home group and sponsorship:
- My current home group is the "181st Street Big Book Study" in Manhattan, New York (Area 49). I have been a member there since 2010 and have held multiple service positions.
- I sponsor 5 active sponsees at the moment, and have sponsored approximately 20 men over the years, with varying lengths of sobriety.
- I was first introduced to sponsorship in 2005 and have had the same primary sponsor since 2008, with whom I maintain regular contact and step work.

Group-level service:
- 2005–2006: Coffee maker and literature person, “Sunrise Beginners” group, Brooklyn, NY.
- 2006–2008: Group treasurer, “Sunrise Beginners” group – handled 7th Tradition funds, maintained group bank account, and ensured contributions were made to district, area, and G.S.O.
- 2008–2010: General Service Representative (G.S.R.), “Sunrise Beginners” group – attended monthly district meetings and area assemblies, gave regular reports to the group, and encouraged group participation in the Conference process.
- 2011–2013: Secretary, “181st Street Big Book Study” – coordinated meeting formats, chair rotation, and ensured adherence to A.A. Traditions.
- 2019–2022: Group chairperson, “181st Street Big Book Study” – oversaw group conscience, facilitated business meetings, and helped navigate the transition between in-person, online, and hybrid formats during and after the COVID-19 pandemic.

District and area service:
- 2008–2010: G.S.R. for my home group in Brooklyn (District 306, SENY/Area 49). Participated in district inventories and worked with the D.C.M. to increase group participation.
- 2010–2012: District 306 Archives Committee member – assisted in collecting group histories, organizing old minutes, and digitizing records.
- 2012–2014: District 306 D.C.M. – chaired monthly district meetings, supported G.S.R.s, and encouraged more frequent communication with the area committee.
- 2015–2017: Area 49 (Southeastern New York) Alternate Chair – helped plan and conduct area assemblies and days of sharing, coordinated with standing committee chairs, and facilitated workshops on the Twelve Concepts for World Service.
- 2017–2019: Area 49 Chairperson – chaired area assemblies and committee meetings; worked closely with the delegate, treasurer, and committee chairs; helped guide the area through discussions on safety in A.A., anonymity in the digital age, and revisiting our area service structure.
- 2020–2022: Area 49 Delegate (Panel 70) – served as the link between my area and the General Service Conference. Attended two Conferences virtually (2020 and 2021) and one in person (2022), gave detailed Conference reports to groups and districts, and organized report-back sessions across the area. Participated on the Conference Committee on Literature in 2020 and 2021 and on the Conference Committee on Policy/Admissions in 2022.

Regional and national service:
- Participated in multiple Regional Forums and National A.A. Technology Workshops.
- Served on an ad hoc regional committee focused on improving communication between area delegates and intergroup/central offices regarding online meetings and digital resources during the pandemic.

Service related to A.A. literature and outreach:
- Helped coordinate a local A.A. writing workshop to encourage members to submit stories for consideration in A.A. literature.
- Participated in carrying the message into hospitals and treatment facilities from 2006–2015, speaking regularly at detox units and rehab programs in Brooklyn and Manhattan.
- Served as a volunteer on the Cooperation with the Professional Community (C.P.C.) committee in Area 49, presenting about A.A. to social workers, clergy, and medical professionals.

Throughout my A.A. service, I have tried to keep the Twelve Traditions and Twelve Concepts at the center of my decisions, seeking group conscience, encouraging minority opinion, and remembering that our ultimate authority is a loving God as He may express Himself in our group conscience. My experience as a delegate in particular gave me a deep appreciation for the work of the General Service Board and A.A. World Services, Inc., and it is that experience that motivates me to offer myself for service as a director.
experience_and_qualifications.occupational_background:
I have spent over 30 years working primarily in the nonprofit and social services sector, with a focus on homelessness, addiction recovery, and community development.

Early career (1990–1998):
- After graduating from U.C. Berkeley with a B.A. in Sociology in 1990, I worked as a case manager at a transitional housing program in Oakland, California. My responsibilities included conducting intake assessments, developing service plans, and coordinating with mental health providers and employment services.
- In 1993, I was promoted to program coordinator, supervising a small team of case managers and overseeing day-to-day operations of the housing program. I gained hands-on experience in budgeting, grant compliance, and basic HR functions.

Transition to New York and graduate studies (1998–2004):
- I moved to New York City in 1998 and took a position as a program manager at a Midtown-based nonprofit providing job readiness training to individuals re-entering the workforce after incarceration or treatment.
- While working full-time, I enrolled in The New School’s M.S. in Nonprofit Management program, which I completed in 2003. My coursework covered governance, strategic planning, fundraising, program evaluation, and ethics in nonprofit leadership.

Mid-career leadership roles (2004–2015):
- 2004–2009: Director of Programs, New Horizons Community Services, Brooklyn, NY. I oversaw a portfolio of housing and employment programs with a combined annual budget of approximately $4.5 million and a staff of 35. I was responsible for program design, contract negotiation, outcome reporting to funders, and collaboration with city agencies.
- 2009–2015: Deputy Executive Director, New Horizons Community Services. In this role, I worked closely with the Executive Director and the Board of Directors on organizational strategy, multi-year budgeting, and major grant proposals. I regularly presented program outcomes and financial summaries to the board and participated in annual independent audits and risk assessments.

Executive leadership and consulting (2015–present):
- 2015–2022: Executive Director, Pathways Recovery Coalition, a mid-sized nonprofit in Manhattan focused on peer-based recovery support, housing stabilization, and workforce development. The organization’s budget grew from approximately $3.2 million to $6.8 million during my tenure. I oversaw all operations, including finance, HR, program development, and external relations.
  - Led a strategic planning process that resulted in a three-year plan with clear metrics and timelines.
  - Guided the organization through the transition to a more robust financial management system and implemented stronger internal controls.
  - Managed relationships with a diverse board of directors, including recruiting new members, developing board orientation materials, and clarifying board/staff roles.
- 2022–present: Independent nonprofit governance and strategy consultant, based in New York City. I work primarily with small- to mid-sized mission-driven organizations, many of which focus on recovery, housing, or mental health.
  - I help boards clarify their roles, strengthen committee structures, and improve communication between boards and executive staff.
  - I facilitate board retreats, organizational inventories, and strategic planning processes.
  - I also assist organizations with policy development, especially around ethics, conflicts of interest, and alignment with mission.

Throughout my career, I have become very familiar with board governance, fiduciary responsibility, financial oversight, and the importance of maintaining a clear distinction between management and governance. While I have never worked for A.A. or any of its corporate entities, my professional life has often run parallel to A.A.’s mission of carrying a message of hope to alcoholics. I am comfortable reading and interpreting financial statements, working collaboratively with diverse stakeholders, and making decisions in a group setting where consensus and principle-based discussion are valued.
experience_and_qualifications.additional_abilities_skills_background_a:
I believe several aspects of my background and skill set could be useful to the A.A. World Services Board:

1. Governance and board experience:
- I have worked closely with nonprofit boards for over two decades, both as an executive reporting to a board and now as a consultant helping boards strengthen their governance practices.
- I am comfortable with board processes such as committee structures, consent agendas, board self-evaluation, and strategic planning.
- I have experience balancing the roles and responsibilities of boards versus staff, which I understand is also an important aspect of A.A. World Services’ relationship with the General Service Board and the General Service Office.
- I have facilitated difficult conversations about mission drift, financial challenges, and leadership transitions, always emphasizing transparency, humility, and adherence to core principles.

2. Financial literacy and organizational stewardship:
- While I am not a CPA, I regularly work with audited financial statements, management letters, and Form 990s. I can read and interpret balance sheets, income statements, and cash flow reports.
- I have helped organizations implement better internal controls, conflict-of-interest policies, and whistleblower protections, all while maintaining a culture of trust and spiritual principles.
- I am comfortable asking detailed questions about budgets and financial assumptions without losing sight of the spiritual nature of A.A.’s primary purpose.

3. Strategic planning and change management:
- I have led and facilitated multiple strategic planning processes, including situations where organizations needed to pivot in response to external changes such as shifts in funding, new regulations, or crises (for example, COVID-19).
- During the pandemic, I helped my own Area and home group navigate the rapid transition to online and hybrid formats, always mindful of A.A.’s Traditions and the importance of inclusivity and accessibility.
- I have a practical understanding of how to implement change while respecting group conscience, minority opinion, and the spiritual nature of our decision-making process.

4. Communication and bilingual ability:
- I am fluent in both English and Spanish, which has been particularly helpful in carrying the message in diverse communities in New York City.
- I have chaired many workshops and presented Conference reports to groups whose primary language is Spanish, working with simultaneous translation when needed and sometimes serving as the translator myself.
- I am an experienced public speaker in both languages and am comfortable presenting complex information (such as Conference actions or governance topics) in a clear, accessible way.

5. Personal experience with alcoholism and recovery:
- I come from a family with a strong history of alcoholism. My father died from alcohol-related liver disease in his early 60s, and I have several relatives who are either in recovery or still struggling.
- My own drinking escalated in my late 20s and early 30s, particularly as my career responsibilities increased. I drank primarily alone, often using alcohol to cope with stress and anxiety.
- By the time I reached A.A., I had alienated many friends, jeopardized my career, and seriously damaged my physical health. I had multiple failed attempts at controlled drinking, including switching drinks, changing schedules, and making resolutions that never lasted.
- A.A. has given me a new way of life: a relationship with a Higher Power of my understanding, a design for living in the Twelve Steps, and a community of people who understand me in a way no one else could.
- This personal experience keeps me grounded. When I think about any potential decision as a director, I instinctively come back to one question: “Will this help us better carry the message to the alcoholic who still suffers?”

6. Experience with diversity, equity, and inclusion in a spiritual context:
- I identify as Latino and grew up in a bilingual household in Los Angeles. I have seen firsthand how language, culture, and economic barriers can keep people from accessing help.
- In my professional work and in A.A., I have been involved in efforts to make meetings and service structures more welcoming to people of color, LGBTQ+ members, and those with limited financial means.
- I have participated in area and district workshops focused on ensuring that our service structures reflect the diversity of our membership and remain open to anyone with a desire to stop drinking.

7. Technology and remote collaboration:
- I am comfortable with common collaboration tools (Zoom, Microsoft Teams, Google Workspace, shared drives, and basic project management platforms).
- I helped my home group set up and maintain a hybrid meeting format, including managing online security, anonymity, and group conscience about recording and screen sharing.
- I have a practical sense of how technology can support A.A.’s work without compromising our spiritual principles or anonymity.

8. Temperament and approach to group decision-making:
- I am generally calm, patient, and willing to listen. My experience as a delegate and area chair taught me the importance of hearing all voices, especially minority opinions, before reaching a decision.
- I am comfortable being in the minority when conscience leads me there, and I am equally comfortable supporting a group decision once it has been made, even if I personally voted another way.
- I try to bring a spirit of humility, humor, and open-mindedness to all my service work. I know that I do not “represent” any special constituency in A.A., and if selected to serve, I would see myself as a trusted servant, not an authority.

Overall, I believe my combination of long-term sobriety, broad A.A. service experience (from coffee maker to delegate), nonprofit governance and management background, bilingual ability, and personal commitment to A.A.’s spiritual principles would allow me to contribute thoughtfully and responsibly to the work of the A.A. World Services Board.
